_comment: Saheeh International footnotes for Al-Fatiha. Brief contextual notes that pair with the Saheeh translation; full footnote set ships in v0.5 via QUL.

verses.1:1: Al-Fatiha — "The Opening" — is also called Umm al-Qur'an (the mother of the Qur'an) and as-Sab' al-Mathani (the seven oft-repeated verses), per the Prophet ﷺ. It is recited in every unit of every prayer.
verses.1:2: The opening praise of the entire Qur'an. "Worlds" (al-'alameen) here is interpreted by classical scholars as everything that exists besides Allah — the worlds of humans, jinn, angels, animals, and creation broadly.
verses.1:3: Both names emphasize Allah's mercy. Ar-Rahman is the wider mercy that encompasses all of creation, believer and disbeliever; Ar-Raheem is the special mercy reserved for the believers in this life and especially in the Hereafter.
verses.1:4: Some readings render this as "King of the Day of Recompense" (Maliki) and others as "Owner" (Maaliki); both are valid Hafs riwayah variants and both are referenced in classical tafsir.
verses.1:5: The grammatical structure (object preceding verb) emphasizes exclusivity — to You alone, not to anyone besides You. This single verse establishes the core of Tawheed al-Uluhiyyah (oneness of worship).
verses.1:6: The Straight Path (as-sirat al-mustaqeem) is interpreted classically as the path of Islam — the path of submission to Allah as defined by His final revelation and His final Messenger ﷺ.
verses.1:7: "Those who have evoked anger" is interpreted in the Sunnah as referring to those who knew the truth and rejected it; "those who are astray" as those who pursued worship without knowledge. The believer asks to be saved from both deviations.
